coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots FRET based sensor for early detection of heart attack in human
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Bhatnagar, Deepika
Kumar, Vanish
Kumar, Ashok
Kaur, Inderpreet
coredata.dc:description: Cardiac immunosensor for early detection of heart attack (myocardial infarction) was developed using amine functionalized graphene quantum dots (afGQDs) conjugated with antibody anti-cardiac Troponin I (anti-cTnI) to detect cardiac marker antigen Troponin I (cTnI) in blood based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between conjugate and graphene (quencher) only in 10min. The anti-cTnI was covalently conjugated to afGQDs through carbodiimide coupling reaction. The conjugate was characterized by zeta potential UV–vis spectroscopy and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The sensing performance of the sensor was studied with respect to changes in the photon count and photoluminescence of GQDs based on interaction of target cTnI with its specific anti-cTnI antibody. The sensor is highly specific and shows negligible response to non-specific antigens. The sensor displayed a linear response to cTnI from 0.001 to 1000ngmL−1 with a limit of detection of 0.192pgmL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cardiac Troponin I sensor
Myocardial infarction
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoluminescence immunoassay on modified afGQDs platforms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge Mr. Manish Kumar for providing CLSM images at IGIB, New Delhi, Dr Avanish Srivastava for FESEM images at NPL, New Delhi and Ms. Rupali Aggarwal, Nexus Central Lab Services (P) Ltd, New Delhi for providing blood serum samples. DB thanks Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) , New Delhi for granting SRF fellowship. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence studies of (a) various precursors at the nanoplatform. (b) The fluorescence quenching spectra of anti-cTnI/afGQDs with increasing concentrations of graphene 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0µgmL−1 from top to bottom. (c) The fluorescence quenching efficiency at varying concentrations of graphene. (d) Immunosensing behavior against specific (cTnI) and non-specific antigen (HsCAg or CA15.3Ag) (inset).

CLSM images at 405nm excitation (a–e) and similar images without excitation in bright field (a′–e′) at magnification of 63×. (a) afGQDs as control, (b) anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe, (c) anti-cTnI/afGQDs/cTnI, (d) anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr and (e) anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr/cTnI. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence recovery spectra of anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr system with increasing concentrations of cTnI antigen 0, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10, 100, 1000ngmL−1 from bottom to top. (b) The linear relationship between the PL intensity of anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr system with increasing log concentrations of cTnI from picomolar to nanomolar.

(a) Bioconjugation of monoclonal antibody anti-cTnI with afGQDs using EDC–NHS chemistry (Fischer, 2010). (b) Schematic mechanism of immunosensing based on specific interaction of anti-cTnI/afGQDs with graphene.
Comparison of limit of detection and total time for various nanomaterial based methods for cTnI detection.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots FRET based sensor for early detection of heart attack in human

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Sector 30-C, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Mall Road, Delhi 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Mall Road, Delhi 110007, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Sector 30-C, Chandigarh 160030, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cardiac immunosensor for early detection of heart attack (myocardial infarction) was developed using amine functionalized graphene quantum dots (afGQDs) conjugated with antibody anti-cardiac Troponin I (anti-cTnI) to detect cardiac marker antigen Troponin I (cTnI) in blood based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between conjugate and graphene (quencher) only in 10min. The anti-cTnI was covalently conjugated to afGQDs through carbodiimide coupling reaction. The conjugate was characterized by zeta potential UV–vis spectroscopy and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The sensing performance of the sensor was studied with respect to changes in the photon count and photoluminescence of GQDs based on interaction of target cTnI with its specific anti-cTnI antibody. The sensor is highly specific and shows negligible response to non-specific antigens. The sensor displayed a linear response to cTnI from 0.001 to 1000ngmL−1 with a limit of detection of 0.192pgmL−1.
Graphene quantum dots FRET based sensor for early detection of heart attack (myocardial infarction).

ce:section.ce:para: Myocardial infarction (MI) commonly called ‘heart attack’ constitutes the major death rates among people suffering from cardiovascular diseases (CVD) (Reichlin et al., 2009). The conventional tools used earlier for the diagnosis of MI in patients was electrocardiogram (ECG) and serial measurements of creatine kinase-isoforms (CK-MB) which were often misleading (Ma et al., 1981; Jaffe et al., 1984). Although, several biomarkers are reported for MI such as Myoglobin (MB), Lactate dehydrogenase (LD) and Troponin (I and T). However, cardiac Troponin I (cTnI) marker, due to its high specificity, has been considered as the ‘gold standard’ for determining MI in patients (Bodor et al., 1992). Troponin I is a structural protein which regulates the heart muscle contraction located on the actin filament of typical myocyte (Adams et al., 1994). On the occurrence of cardiac injury (MI), cTnI is released into the bloodstream (Sasse et al., 1993). Patients are risk-stratified with 99th percentile reference limit of 0.04ngmL−1 derived from cTnI concentration. The death risk from MI is directly associated to Troponin I level in serum which increases drastically to 0.7–1.4ngmL−1 within 3–12h on the onset of MI chest pain, peak at 24–48h and return to baseline over 5–14 days (Vichairuangthum et al., 2006; Mahajan and Jarolim, 2011). Presently, several methods are reported for detecting the levels of cTnI in blood serum based on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Tate, 2008), colorimetric procedures (Lee et al., 2015), electrochemiluminescence (Xu et al., 2015), fluorescence techniques (Kar et al., 2012), impedimetric method (Periyakeruppan et al., 2013), electro-chemical method (Guo et al., 2005) and chemi-resistive immunosensor (Rajesh et al., 2013). Although, these methods are moderately sensitive and specific but time consuming, expensive, tedious and require sophisticated equipments which are not available in each hospital and diagnostic laboratories. One of the sensitive and most reliable techniques which is widely used in biomolecular detection is based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). A FRET based sensor requires excellent donor–acceptor pair to improve its efficiency and performance. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are planar nanocrystals of graphene (Gr) with size less than 100nm with higher surface-to-volume ratio (Peng et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2012) used in extraordinary applications (Park and Ruoff, 2009; Eda and Chhowalla, 2010; Loh et al., 2010; Tripathi et al., 2013) in bioimaging (Zhu et al., 2011), biosensing (Li et al., 2013a; Lu et al., 2014), organic photovoltaics (Baker and Baker, 2010), solar cell applications (Li et al., 2013b; Gao et al., 2014) and diagnostics (Ogaidi et al., 2014). In contrast to conventional organic dyes, metal nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), GQDs are better due to strong stable fluorescence and biocompatibility (Zhao et al., 2013).

Here, we report a simple, rapid and sensitive FRET based immunosensor for detection of cTnI using GQDs and Gr. The capturing monoclonal antibody cardiac Troponin I (anti-cTnI) was covalently conjugated with amine functionalized graphene quantum dots (afGQDs) to form anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe. The fluorescence activity of the nanoprobe was further investigated with Gr that results in quenching of the fluorescence intensity of anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe. Finally, quantification of target antigen (cTnI) was achieved by adding cTnI on anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr, expelling stacked Gr apart from GQDs nanoprobe and resulting in fluorescence recovery. The reaction between cardiac antibody and functionalized GQDs with sensing mechanism of FRET is shown in Scheme 1.

GQDs and single layer graphene (Gr) were procured from ACS material, USA. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were received from Sigma, USA and Pierce Germany, respectively. Antibody (anti-cTnI) and antigen (cTnI) were procured from Abcam, UK. 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid monohydrate (MES) was obtained from Merck, India. Liquor ammonia, di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4) were purchased from Qualigens, India. Hepatitis C antigen (HsCAg) and cancer antigen (CA15.3Ag) were obtained from Genxbio Health Sciences (P) Ltd. India. Troponin I Elisa tests kit was obtained from Calbiotech Inc., Spring Valley, CA, USA. Ultra centrifugal filter tubes (Amicon Ultra-4) were received from Merck Millipore, Germany. All other chemicals were analytical grade.

For preparation of afGQDs (Tetsuka et al., 2012), 6mL aqueous solution of GQDs (1mgmL−1) was mixed with 6mL ammonia solution (25% in water) and stirred for 1h at room temperature. The solution was transferred to the Teflon vessel and heated at 200°C for 10h and cooled at room temperature. The solution was further heated up to 100°C for 1h to vaporize excess ammonia followed by dialysis in water.

Activation of antibody was carried out by incubating 990μl of anti-cTnI with 10μL of EDC–NHS solution (0.4mg EDC and 1.1mg NHS in 100µL of 0.1M MES buffer, pH 4.7) for 15min at room temperature with constant stirring. Activated anti-cTnI was incubated with afGQDs solution (1:1v/v) for 1h at 37°C to form anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate. After that, the conjugate was washed five times with PBS (0.1M sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 containing 0.9% NaCl) and free unconjugated molecules were removed using Amicon Ultra-4 centrifugal tubes (absorbs unconjugated molecules) by ultracentrifugation [shown in Scheme 1(a)].

In order to detect the target antigen (cTnI), the quenching effect and fluorescence recovery was observed on anti-cTnI/afGQDs in following steps: 500µL of anti-cTnI/afGQDs suspension was taken in eppendorf tube and mixed with 100µL of graphene solution (3µgmL−1) to observe the change in photoluminescence (PL) intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360nm at room temperature. After that, 100µL specific (cTnI) or non-specific antigens (HsCAg and CA15.3Ag) were added into the anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr solution (600µL) and made final volume to 1mL with PBS, pH 7.4. After incubation of 10min, the PL intensity of the mixture was measured at 360nm excitation wavelength. The specificity test with cTnI and non-specific antigens were also performed in human blood serum (the blood was drawn from normal person and kept at room temperature for 5min to clot and centrifuged at 5000×g for 5min to get clear serum). The blood serum does not contain blood cell (RBC, WBC and clotting factor fibrinogen).
Different antigen concentrations (1ngmL−1) of cTnI, HsCAg and CA15.3Ag were prepared in serum (pH 7.4) and PBS (pH 7.4). The PL intensity was measured as described above.

The mechanism of immunosensing between GQDs conjugated anti-cTnI and Gr for detecting cTnI is shown in Scheme 1(b). GQDs absorb UV radiations (360nm) and emit blue radiations (437nm) which can be absorbed by the Gr sheet (quencher). The anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe was incubated with Gr to facilitate the adsorption of anti-cTnI/afGQDs on the surface of Gr through electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction (Chen et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2013). On excitation, the energy (photons) emitted by the donor gets transferred non-radiatively to Gr due to dipole–dipole interaction (Saini et al., 2006). The self-assembled anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr can act as an immunosensor for the detection of target cTnI. Further, addition of cTnI, it binds to the antibody (anti-cTnI) due to the specific antibody–antigen interaction to form anti-cTnI/afGQDs/cTnI, which effectively increased the distance between anti-cTnI/afGQDs/cTnI conjugate and Gr surface (detachment of π–π interaction between GQDs conjugate and Gr sheet), thus hindering the FRET process and then producing a restoration (recovery) of fluorescence.
The ELISA was performed using Troponin I ELISA kit (as described in S4, Supplementary Information).

The anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe formation was characterized with zeta potential studies and UV–vis spectroscopy described in Fig. S1. The surface morphology of afGQDs and modified conjugates were characterized by FESEM shown in Fig. S2 (details are in Supplementary Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence immunoassay on modified afGQDs platforms
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different stages of GQDs modifications for fabrication of immunosensor are shown in Fig. 1. The PL spectrum of pristine GQDs extensively shows emission peak at 437nm with a shoulder peak at 414nm [Fig. 1(a)]. After the amine functionalization of GQDs, both the emission peaks were shifted to 446nm with a shoulder peak at 423nm (red shift). The emission peak of anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate was further shifted to 457nm (red shift) along with an increase in intensity corresponding to that of afGQDs at the excitation wavelength of 360nm. The carry-over shift in afGQDs and anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate indicates their successful synthesis. To understand the fluorescent behavior of anti-cTnI/afGQDs complex, a definite quantity of target cTnI was added to the anti-cTnI/afGQDs probe to form anti-cTnI/afGQDs/cTnI complex. On addition of target antigen cTnI, the peak position was not shifted but slightly reduced due to specific interactions between anti-cTnI/afGQDs and cTnI. Although, emission band shapes were not much different but narrow in intensity and peak suggesting the formation of anti-cTnI/ afGQDs/cTnI complex. Further, on addition of Gr to the anti-cTnI/afGQDs nanoprobe, the PL intensity was significantly decreased due to the adsorption of anti-cTnI/afGQDs on the surface of Gr resulting in quenching of the fluorescence (switch ‘off’ FRET). Fig. 1(b) and (c) shows PL intensities at different concentrations of Gr and steadily decreases with increasing concentrations of Gr. In terms of quenching efficiency, it increases with increase in concentration of Gr. When the concentration of Gr reached to 3.0µgmL−1, the PL intensity remains unchanged indicating completion of reaction between anti-cTnI/afGQDs and Gr. Thus, 3.0µgmL−1 was chosen as optimal concentration of Gr for this system. In Fig. 1(d), on addition of target cTnI (0.1ngmL−1) to the anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr, the antigen–antibody (anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr/cTnI) immune-complex formed, resulting in recovery of fluorescence. The formation of this complex leads to separation between conjugated afGQDs and Gr and hence switching ‘on’ the FRET. Besides, on addition of non-specific antigens, HsCAg and CA15.3Ag in PBS (pH 7.4) as well as in serum (pH 7.4) to the above system, there was no switching ‘on’ fluorescence recovery in the spectra of anti-cTnI/afGQD/Gr/HsCAg and anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr/CA15.3Ag [Fig. 1(d) inset]. These results suggest the specificity of the sensor only with cTnI antigen. The specific switch ‘on-off-on’ mode in fluorescence based on FRET between conjugated afGQDs and Gr is investigated as a sensitive immunosensor for quantitative analysis of cTnI.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was employed (S3, Supplementary) for the fluorescence imaging performance and photon count generated at different nanoplatforms ( Fig. 2 and Table S1). All modified platforms were observed for FRET under an excitation of 405nm [Fig. 2(a–e)] along with same images in bright field without excitation [Fig. 2(a′–e′)]. afGQDs (control) showed planar sheet in bright field [Fig. 2(a′)] and gives blue fluorescence upon excitation [Fig. 2(a)]. The brightness in fluorescence (with calculated photon count in Table S1) slightly reduced after the formation of anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate [Fig. 2(b)]. There was no significant difference in fluorescence of anti-cTnI/afGQDs/cTnI complex [Fig. 2(c)] except a further slight reduction in photon count which also confirms the spectra obtained from PL observations [Fig. 1(d)]. After incubating the anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate with Gr, there was considerable fluorescence quenching in the GQDs fluorescence [Fig. 2(d)]. However, the bright field image of the anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr [Fig. 2(d′)] showed the flakes of Gr masked the GQDs conjugate indicating the quenching in the fluorescence. The stacked Gr displaced from the surface of anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate upon the addition of cTnI clearly observed in Fig. 2(e′), causing recovery in the fluorescence intensity [Fig. 2(e)]. Similar results were also found in PL spectra of all types of fluorescent derivatives of afGQDs conjugate [Fig. 1(d)]. The mean values of fluorescence intensity (photon count) at different modified platforms of afGQDs are summarized in Table S1 which clearly indicates the changes at different modifications of sensing platform.

Under the optimal conditions, various concentrations of antigen (in blood serum) were used into the anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr nanoplatform solution for quantitative estimation of cTnI. On addition of cTnI into anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr, the PL intensity was recovered after formation of anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr/cTnI [ Fig. 3(a)]. With the subsequent addition of cTnI, due to formation of large number of anti-cTnI/afGQDs/Gr/cTnI complexes, escaping Gr from the surface, resulting in enhanced PL intensity. The linear relationship between PL intensity (I) and logarithm of cTnI concentrations is shown in Fig. 3(b) (calibration graph) which can be expressed as, I=6208.37×logcTnI+2901.62 with regression coefficient (R 2) 0.9909. The lower limit of detection (LOD) of cTnI was found 0.192pgmL−1 (S/N=4) and detection time was 10min which are lower than reported earlier ( Table 1). The linear range was found to be 1.0pgmL−1–1.0ngmL−1. The accuracy of the present FRET method was compared with ELISA which showed good correlation (regression coefficient, R 2=0.998) and y=0.9897x+0.1091 [Fig. S4]. The coefficients of variation (% CV) within and between assays were 0.57 and 0.52 respectively, showed good repeatability and reliability (precision) of the present FRET method (Table S2). The anti-cTnI/afGQDs conjugate was found stable for 12 months at 4°C with only 5% loss in initial PL intensity. GQD is very stable even at room temperature for several months (Peng et al., 2012).

The loss in PL intensity on storage of conjugate may be due to anti-cTnI antibody. However, antibody (anti-cTnI) and afGQDs can also be stored separately at 4˚C and mixed at the time of the experiment. The response time of the sensor was 10s and the total time for detection of cTnI from proposed immunosensor is required only 10min. This is the most efficient and rapid detection of cTnI using FRET between GQDs conjugate and Gr to achieve specific and quantitative detection.

In summary, GQDs based immunosensor is better than earlier reported methods due to higher specificity (cTnI), lower limit of detection (0.192pgmL−1) and lesser time (10min) for the detection of cTnI in myocardial infarction patients. The present method was validated with the different concentrations of cTnI in blood serum [Fig. 3(b)] which is found in early stage of myocardial infarction causing heart attack.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge Mr. Manish Kumar for providing CLSM images at IGIB, New Delhi, Dr Avanish Srivastava for FESEM images at NPL, New Delhi and Ms. Rupali Aggarwal, Nexus Central Lab Services (P) Ltd, New Delhi for providing blood serum samples. DB thanks Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), New Delhi for granting SRF fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.12.083.
